one_liner: One promo code to win every Super Bowl product.
search_doc:
One promo code to win every Super Bowl product.
Mechanisms: platform-jailbreak, competitor-piggyback, real-time-advertising-hijack, promo-code-as-content, media-as-product
Idea: Every time another brand's Super Bowl ad debuted, DoorDash added their products into one online cart, so the world could keep track in real-time. Then after four straight quarters of brand, influencer, and internet engagement during the game, the Super Bowl ad aired featuring an incredibly long promo code that you had to enter correctly for a chance to get everything advertised.
Insight: DoorDash was renowned for restaurant delivery, but not for the millions of other products now offered on the app. So during the Super Bowl, when the other brands ran their own ads, DoorDash saw an opportunity to prove that it could now deliver pretty much anything by delivering all the products, from all the other Super Bowl ads, all to one person.

extracted.tagline: Delivering everything advertised from every Super Bowl commercial.
extracted.insight: DoorDash was renowned for restaurant delivery, but not for the millions of other products now offered on the app. So during the Super Bowl, when the other brands ran their own ads, DoorDash saw an opportunity to prove that it could now deliver pretty much anything by delivering all the products, from all the other Super Bowl ads, all to one person.
extracted.idea: Every time another brand's Super Bowl ad debuted, DoorDash added their products into one online cart, so the world could keep track in real-time. Then after four straight quarters of brand, influencer, and internet engagement during the game, the Super Bowl ad aired featuring an incredibly long promo code that you had to enter correctly for a chance to get everything advertised.
execution: DoorDash branded car / pickup truck OOH placements
press_coverage: NBC — Caught The Internet By Storm
ABC News — DoorDash Crushed The Super Bowl

inferred.context: Super Bowl ad slots are the most expensive and crowded media moment of the year, where every brand fights to be remembered.
inferred.one_liner: One promo code to win every Super Bowl product.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Hijacked the entire Super Bowl ad break by turning every other brand's commercial into a DoorDash delivery; 11.9 billion impressions and 8 million entries reframed DoorDash from food-only to everything delivery.

visual.style_description: Cinematic dusk landscape with a giant red DoorDash bag at the center surrounded by mountains of branded products from real Super Bowl ads; press quotes hover above, three large metric blocks anchor the middle, and the bottom strip shows OOH activations and bodega-style takeovers.

idea.rationale: Treating the entire Super Bowl ad break as DoorDash's product catalogue — and turning every rival commercial into a line item in a single live cart unlocked by one absurdly long promo code — is a genuine playbook-rewrite for the most expensive media moment of the year. It is category-redefining because it converts competitors' paid GRPs into proof of DoorDash's own product breadth, not a parody or a side stunt.

strategy.rationale: DoorDash is the US on-demand delivery marketplace whose business has expanded from restaurant food into a broad commerce platform; the mission-fit is exact, because the campaign's entire job is to prove the brand 'now delivers everything'. The stated insight — that consumers still associate DoorDash with restaurants despite the platform's new breadth — is a plausible perception gap, though the board does not cite share-of-mind data to anchor it. Audacity to claim the Super Bowl ad break itself sits comfortably inside DoorDash's challenger posture.

execution.rationale: Cross-discipline orchestration is unusually broad for a single-spot brief: a real-time cart updated quarter-by-quarter as rival ads aired, OOH wraps on DoorDash trucks, a Popeyes parody execution, bodega-style installations, a dedicated landing page, and a closing in-game spot carrying the unlock code. The mechanic is native to the Super Bowl's quarter-structure rather than a film campaign retrofitted to social, and 76 official and unofficial brand partnerships indicate the orchestration scaled in-flight rather than being pre-rigged.

impact.rationale: The 11.9B impressions figure is entrant-reported and unaudited, but 8 million submissions of the promo code is a hard interaction count tied to a specific call-to-action, which is a stronger behavioural proxy than reach alone. The 2,468 individual products delivered demonstrates the campaign actually fulfilled its core claim — 'we deliver everything' — rather than asserting it. Press citations from NBC, Time and ABC News are entrant-curated quotes rather than verified outcomes, and the board does not show a sales lift or category-share shift, which is the one missing direct business-outcome a marketplace would normally surface.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because it turned the Super Bowl ad break — every rival's commercial included — into a live DoorDash product catalogue redeemable by one promo code, a structural reinvention of how a brand can use the most crowded media moment of the year.

one_liner: An anonymous granny becomes Spain's biggest subway star.
search_doc:
An anonymous granny becomes Spain's biggest subway star.
Mechanisms: anonymous-as-celebrity, ooh-as-narrative, real-person-cliffhanger, media-as-product, curiosity-driven-campaign
Idea: Turn a single anonymous 100-year-old neighbour, Marina Prieto, into the unwitting hero of JCDecaux's subway billboards and let curiosity from commuters and the press make her — and the medium — go viral.
Insight: Idea — We used hundreds of space-brands feeds to build a plan: Marina's IG growing fast. More than 850 spaces showed Marina's pictures growing 'visiting fans to' taking a trip without further explanation. No paid ads, no plug, just word-of-mouth.

extracted.tagline: The unknown 100-year-old granny who revived Spain's subway ads.
extracted.context: In 2023, subway commuters in Spain decreased by 7%. Because of this, JCDecaux's adv[…] decline in their company. Furthermore, aesthetic-conscious brands that the audience [...] truly works, encouraging them to reinvest.
extracted.insight: Idea — We used hundreds of space-brands feeds to build a plan: Marina's IG growing fast. More than 850 spaces showed Marina's pictures growing 'visiting fans to' taking a trip without further explanation. No paid ads, no plug, just word-of-mouth.
extracted.execution: Marina went viral. What started as the subway resulted in real ranks, brand-conversations, [...] TV calls and even smart science. As a result, JCDecaux didn't just secure the same place in the 1990s. Proving the power of the medium and reinvesting brands in stories of again.
press_coverage: SER — Marina Prieto from unknown to a real influencer
EL ESPAÑOL — All of Spain is asking: who is Marina Prieto?
Antena 3 — An advertising campaign that changed everything
EL PAÍS — Who is this lady?

inferred.idea: Turn a single anonymous 100-year-old neighbour, Marina Prieto, into the unwitting hero of JCDecaux's subway billboards and let curiosity from commuters and the press make her — and the medium — go viral.
inferred.one_liner: An anonymous granny becomes Spain's biggest subway star.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Made the subway billboard itself the star by turning an unknown 100-year-old into a national mystery; Marina's Instagram grew 39,285%, 185 new brands signed on and JCDecaux logged record bookings, reversing a 7% commuter dip into doubled media investment.

visual.style_description: Hero shot of an actual subway platform with a giant lit ad of an elderly woman in a red sweater and sun hat, framed by the bench and tiled tracks of the Madrid metro; small phone mockups of the viral Instagram feed sit on either side, with dense left text columns and a six-column metric strip plus thumbnail row across the bottom.

idea.rationale: Making an anonymous 100-year-old neighbour the recurring face of subway billboards — with no brand, product, or explanation — turns the OOH panel itself into a serialised narrative and forces the public to investigate the medium. It is original because the billboard is no longer a vessel for an advertiser's message but the message itself, and the protagonist is the most under-cast figure in advertising (a centenarian woman) rather than a celebrity.

strategy.rationale: JCDecaux is the French-founded global out-of-home media owner whose business depends on convincing advertisers that subway and street panels still command attention; running the campaign on its own inventory to re-prove the medium's pulling power is exact mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The stated insight — a 7% decline in Spanish subway commuters in 2023 dragging on advertiser confidence — anchors the brief in a real business problem, though the underlying perception gap among aesthetic-conscious brands is asserted rather than evidenced on the board. The audacity of a media owner self-funding cultural curiosity around its own panels is on-brand for a category that rarely advertises itself.

execution.rationale: Cross-discipline orchestration covers more than 850 subway spaces showing Marina with no copy or logo, a paired Instagram account that the public discovered organically, and a press loop that pulled in SER, El Español, Antena 3 and El País asking 'who is this lady?'. No paid social or influencer plug was layered on top, which is unusual restraint and means the OOH-to-earned-press chain is the actual mechanic rather than a budget-driven amplification. The campaign is natively outdoor — it cannot exist in any other channel without losing the cliffhanger.

impact.rationale: The 39,285% Instagram growth and 13,405% engagement figures are unanchored — 39,285% of an unknown starting follower count is mathematically large but the absolute base is the load-bearing number and it is not disclosed. The harder business-outcome signals are stronger: 185 new brands onboarded, doubled media investment and record bookings for JCDecaux directly answer the campaign's revenue brief, which is the outcome a media owner cares about. Press coverage across four major Spanish outlets is entrant-curated but plausible given the campaign's narrative structure.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because a media owner used its own subway panels to make an anonymous centenarian into a national mystery and turned that earned curiosity into 185 new advertisers and doubled media investment — proof-by-product that the medium still works.

one_liner: The video game CV becomes a real manager job.
search_doc:
The video game CV becomes a real manager job.
Mechanisms: gaming-as-real-credential, fan-into-protagonist, category-recruitment-hack, brand-as-employer, platform-jailbreak
Idea: Recruit a real-world football manager for non-league Bromley FC from the most experienced Football Manager video game players, turning gaming hours into a verified CV.
Insight: No football fan is alone in thinking they could manage a team. Many spend hundreds of hours a month engaging in football.

extracted.tagline: Blurring the lines between simulation and the real world.
extracted.context: Football is flourishing in the Premier League, the richest league in the world. They have the best talent, and data, which is transforming the sport. But four leagues below, in the National League, teams are struggling and being left behind.
extracted.insight: No football fan is alone in thinking they could manage a team. Many spend hundreds of hours a month engaging in football.
execution: For the launch of Football Manager 2024, Xbox helped the National League tap into a base of silent (gaming) Football Manager UK and Ireland users
Used the data to replicate the real game, including game tactics and finding a recruitment tool to hire a top gaming talent as the new tactician for professional team Bromley FC
Built gaming community engaged with the run; Xbox aired views on the job ad, and one gamer got the job
He posted FC achieve their highest league position and earned defeats in the league
Documentary changed perception of how football talent is recruited
metrics: 190% increase in FM gamers on Xbox
Best season in Bromley's 130 year history

inferred.idea: Recruit a real-world football manager for non-league Bromley FC from the most experienced Football Manager video game players, turning gaming hours into a verified CV.
inferred.one_liner: The video game CV becomes a real manager job.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Bridged simulation and reality by handing a real National League manager job to a Football Manager player; 1.5B impressions, 190% rise in FM gamers on Xbox, and Bromley's best season in 130 years made it the most-played FM ever.

visual.style_description: Cinematic photo hero of a young Black man in a stadium seat holding an Xbox controller as if managing the pitch behind him, anchoring an Xbox / Football Manager case board; right column stacks vertical metric callouts, top row carries press quotes, and a bottom strip shows documentary stills and press logos.

idea.rationale: Converting hours played in Football Manager into a verifiable CV — and using that CV to hand a real National League manager role at Bromley FC to a gamer — collapses the simulation-reality gap in a way the football industry has not done before. It is genuinely original because the brand's product output (the game's saved-game data) becomes the recruitment instrument for a real professional club, not a marketing surrogate.

strategy.rationale: Xbox owns Football Manager, the deep-simulation football game whose mission is 'be a real football manager in your living room'; literally placing one of its players in a dugout is the most exact mission-fit available to the brand. The stated insight — fans believe they could manage a team and spend hundreds of hours doing so — is observably true within the FM community, though 'hundreds of hours a month' is the campaign's own figure rather than externally sourced. The contrast between the Premier League's data-rich top and the National League's data-poor bottom is well-framed and gives the work a credible cultural foothold.

execution.rationale: The orchestration moves from a real recruitment ad placed in football channels, to a community-judged shortlist drawn from FM gamer data, to onboarding the winner at Bromley FC, to a documentary capturing his real-world season — each phase load-bearing rather than decorative. Channel choice is native: the campaign lives where its audience already plays and reads about football (Match of the Day, BBC, The Athletic, FourFourTwo, BT Sports) rather than on broad-reach paid social. The amplification loop ran on the actual results of the season, not on pre-shot creative.

impact.rationale: The 190% increase in FM gamers on Xbox is a direct business outcome for the platform and tied to a specific SKU, which is the kind of metric the brand actually answers for; '1.5B impressions' is entrant-reported and unverified, but it sits alongside the harder number rather than carrying the case alone. 'Best season in Bromley's 130-year history' is verifiable from public football records and gives the campaign a real-world proof-point that survives outside the case film. 'Most played FM ever' is plausible but framed as a superlative without a comparison cohort on the board.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because it dissolved the simulation-reality boundary by giving a Football Manager player a real National League dugout — a structural product-as-credential mechanic that delivered Bromley's best season in 130 years and a 190% lift in Xbox FM gamers.

one_liner: Three degrees warmer freezers, 17.7M tons less carbon.
search_doc:
Three degrees warmer freezers, 17.7M tons less carbon.
Mechanisms: industry-norm-broken-deliberately, open-source-research-as-platform, coalition-as-campaign, competitor-collaboration, category-rewrite
Idea: There are many different companies in the frozen food supply chain. DP World knew that to persuade them all to make the move to -15, competitors would have to become partners. So they made the academic research data freely available and formed a neutral, unbranded coalition which the world's largest shipping companies, hauliers, warehouses and food producers were happy to join.
Insight: Industry-wide habits like -18°C frozen shipping are never re-examined because no single brand benefits from changing them; a neutral coalition built on open science can.

extracted.tagline: For the last century we have shipped food at the globally recognised standard of -18 degrees Celsius. Every degree with a significant impact on global warming. But does it have to be that cold?
extracted.context: As the world's temperatures continue to rise, established business practices need to be challenged. DP World, global logistics providers, challenged the convention of shipping food at -18 degrees and commissioned a group of independent academics to find out if this long held practice could be transformed to help a warming planet. After months of intensive research, we got the world-changing answer. The temperature inside the shipping containers could be raised to -15. This would be safe for the food inside the containers, and for the world outside… transformative.
extracted.idea: There are many different companies in the frozen food supply chain. DP World knew that to persuade them all to make the move to -15, competitors would have to become partners. So they made the academic research data freely available and formed a neutral, unbranded coalition which the world's largest shipping companies, hauliers, warehouses and food producers were happy to join.
execution: Commissioned independent academic research on raising frozen-shipping temperatures
Published the data openly to the industry
Onboarded 17 of the world's largest shipping companies and 60% of global container shipping
metrics: 60% of the global container shipping industry joined up
+11% increase in brand trust for DPWorld
17.7M million tons — the carbon saving from a 3 degree change
press_coverage: Ex-Prime Minister of Denmark — It's powerful to see competitors come together on something momentous, which doesn't require new tech, just collaboration
Bloomberg — Raising temperature of frozen foods by just three degrees from -18°C to -15°C can slash carbon emissions

inferred.insight: Industry-wide habits like -18°C frozen shipping are never re-examined because no single brand benefits from changing them; a neutral coalition built on open science can.
inferred.one_liner: Three degrees warmer freezers, 17.7M tons less carbon.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Persuaded 60% of global container shipping and 17 of the world's largest carriers to raise frozen-food temperatures from -18°C to -15°C, unlocking 17.7M tons of annual carbon savings without new machinery.

visual.style_description: Hero is a glowing Earth at the centre of a dark clock face flanked by a frost-covered grey fruit and a frost-covered green pepper, with a large overlay headline 'The Move to -15°'; press logos and quote pull-outs run along the top, three text columns (Background / Idea / Results) sit below, and three large outlined metric blocks anchor the foot.

idea.rationale: Challenging the century-old -18°C frozen-shipping standard with peer-reviewed evidence — and then deliberately stripping the brand off the coalition so competitors can join — is an industry-norm-broken-deliberately mechanic at infrastructure scale. The originality is structural rather than aesthetic: the 'creative' is the unbranded coalition itself, not an ad about it, which is rare and category-redefining for a B2B logistics brand.

strategy.rationale: DP World is the Dubai-headquartered global ports, logistics and supply-chain operator whose mission is to make global trade flow; commissioning the academic case to raise frozen temperatures and then convening the rest of the supply chain is core-business advocacy, not borrowed purpose. The strategic call to remove DP World branding from the coalition shows real WHY-discipline — the brand consciously trades short-term attribution for long-term industry change, which is the only way a single carrier could move a 100-year-old standard. The premise is data-backed by named independent academic research and corroborated by Bloomberg's reporting on the three-degree carbon math.

execution.rationale: Multi-discipline orchestration runs from commissioned academic research, to open publication of the dataset, to formation of a neutral coalition, to a COP28 launch designed for policy and press visibility, to onboarding of 17 of the world's largest shipping companies. Channels are precisely matched to a B2B-and-policy target — Forbes, Bloomberg, ACW, GreenFleet, RAC and Climate Samurai rather than broad consumer media — and the COP28 stage is the right venue for legitimising a cross-industry standard. The campaign is built as infrastructure, not a film.

impact.rationale: 60% of the global container-shipping industry signing on is a hard adoption metric tied to a named denominator, which is the strongest possible proof for a coalition campaign — and the kind of business outcome (industry behaviour change) the brief actually targets. 17.7M tons of annual carbon saving is the entrant's projection from the three-degree change rather than a measured year-one result, so it should be read as modelled rather than realised. The +11% brand trust lift is unbased — 11% of what cohort, measured how, is not disclosed — and the campaign's endorsement by a former Danish PM is a credible voice but ultimately a quote rather than an outcome.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because DP World walked away from its own logo to convene a neutral, open-science coalition that moved 60% of global container shipping off a 100-year-old frozen-food temperature standard — industry behaviour redefined at infrastructure scale rather than advertised.
